cot_prefix: Okay, let's tackle this question. The user is asking why a novelist can expect the publisher to print the manuscript exactly as written. The options are A through D. First, I need to look back at the passage provided. The relevant part mentions that a well-established novelist might deliver the manuscript and expect it to be printed as written. The passage says that the novelist keeps absolute control over the text until it's published and received. It also notes that while editors might advise revisions, the novelist isn't obligated to follow that advice. So if the writer refuses, no one is surprised. Looking at the options: A) Because the novelist keeps absolute control over his text. This seems directly related to the passage. The text says the novelist has absolute control until publication. B) Because paperback novels are portable and adaptable. This is mentioned, but it's about the format's advantages, not about the control over the text. C) Because the novel is limited to a single channel—writing. This is also mentioned, but it's explaining how novels can go anywhere without cost constraints. It doesn't directly address the control of the text. D) Because the novelist is seldom advised by editors. The passage says they may be advised, but they can refuse. It's not about being seldom advised but rather the control the novelist
